story:
Gina, 34, is back in her childhood cul-de-sac after a breakup she'd rather not explain. To keep busy (and visible), she takes a noon job walking Fiona, the Pattersons' tiny white Shih Tzu—awkward, because Florence knows Gina once slept with her husband. Gina jogs up in pink spandex, skips the little sweater laid out for the dog, and lets Fiona tug her toward a not-so-boring route: a dead-end that opens onto a bluff, a lone bench, soft fur, a brief pocket of peace. Gina thinks maybe she could be someone gentler, someone who paints again.

Florence, 41, has built her days around vigilance—against humiliation, against hope. Nine years after the affair, she lives in a private maze of staying: cooking, folding, pretending, watching pronouns and phone dings. Her fury simmers; her face betrays it. Tonight, Fiona is missing, and Florence's terror blooms into obsession. Todd had been planning a surprise weekend away—bags already in the trunk—but Florence refused to leave without the dog. She breaks their unspoken rule and detours into his office. On the camera feed, she sees Gina arrive at noon, chipper, key in hand, and leave with Fiona bare against the cold. Disrespect. Maybe something darker. In Todd's email: a new message, cryptic—"RP 1010 Wallace 1900 – G." Rendezvous point. Time-stamped yesterday. Florence decides Todd met Gina last night. Shame and fear tangle. Her baby—Fiona—is gone. Her marriage is a ruin dressed up as a home.

Todd, 48, combs the neighborhood after midnight calling for Fiona, sure Gina couldn't have gotten far. He's learned to make up for anniversaries by pampering Florence off-cycle; it never repaired trust. Still, he loves her. Their best ceasefire lives in the dog's soft presence. And there's a threat he won't name—men sent for his wife because of his own dirty deals. He did something he shouldn't, he tells himself, a life for a life, all to keep Florence safe. Now he needs the dog back and a way out. Under a bench he finds Fiona shivering. He wraps her in his jacket, hurries away from what he recognizes too easily as a crime scene, and begins to plan flight—and disguise—for himself and Florence.

Dominic, 55, waits off a deer trail, hands sweating in gloves he can't afford to drop. His mother's cancer bills drown him; the mysterious "G" who hired him knows it. The job is simple and terrible: kill Florence Patterson, 41, no kids, who walks her dog at noon. All he was given was an address and a time: redhead, Shih Tzu, noon sharp. At 12 on the dot, a redheaded jogger bounces to the door, pulls out a key, and leaves with the dog. Same red hair. Same dog. Same time. He doesn't question it. He shadows them to the dead-end bluff where a lone bench overlooks the drop. His first shot goes wide when she crouches to pet the dog. He steadies, breath held. For Mom.

Gina lingers at the bluff she never noticed as a child. Cloud light sifts through branches; the wind smells like a second chance. She tightens Fiona's leash, imagines the Pattersons surviving, maybe even growing; a whispered rumor says Florence is pregnant and hasn't told Todd yet. Gina wants to paint again—sun rays bursting, abundance returning. Her breath slows. In. Out. In—

Later, Fiona trembles under the bench on the bluff, and Todd carries her home. Florence, staring at the cracked mirror of her marriage and a stray email she misreads as betrayal, doesn't know the worst thing has already happened. Wrong target, right damage. A family built on secrets tilts toward flight, while the woman who once cracked it open finally finds a view worth keeping—and never gets to keep it.
